6554318c4579e16ae2938e2e.question: In the mid-18th century, a Dr. Brownrigg from North Carolina proposed that the American colonies could use this crop to replace imports of olive oil, a suggestion that was not taken up. Samuel Crocker Cobb was an important trader in this crop in the late 19th century, whose diaries detail an unpredictable trade in this crop with Gorée. Sacks of this crop were piled into massive (*) pyramids by traders such as the one-time richest man in West Africa, Alhassan Dantata. Frank Samuel led a project centered on this crop that exemplifies a “second colonial occupation” outlined by Anthony Low and John Lonsdale. Following its introduction, this crop superseded a similar plant commonly named for the (*) Bambara people in importance in West Africa. The Spanish word for this crop comes from the Nahuatl word tlācacahuatl [tlah-cah-CAH-wattle]. Converted Sherman tanks called “shervicks” were utilized in a failed attempt to industrially produce this crop in British Tanzania. For 10 points, what is this legume that comes in Virginia and Valencia varieties and is commonly boiled in the South?
6554318c4579e16ae2938e2e.question_sanitized: In the mid-18th century, a Dr. Brownrigg from North Carolina proposed that the American colonies could use this crop to replace imports of olive oil, a suggestion that was not taken up. Samuel Crocker Cobb was an important trader in this crop in the late 19th century, whose diaries detail an unpredictable trade in this crop with Goree. Sacks of this crop were piled into massive (*) pyramids by traders such as the one-time richest man in West Africa, Alhassan Dantata. Frank Samuel led a project centered on this crop that exemplifies a "second colonial occupation" outlined by Anthony Low and John Lonsdale. Following its introduction, this crop superseded a similar plant commonly named for the (*) Bambara people in importance in West Africa. The Spanish word for this crop comes from the Nahuatl word tlacacahuatl [tlah-cah-CAH-wattle]. Converted Sherman tanks called "shervicks" were utilized in a failed attempt to industrially produce this crop in British Tanzania. For 10 points, what is this legume that comes in Virginia and Valencia varieties and is commonly boiled in the South?
6554318c4579e16ae2938e2e.answer: peanuts [or groundnut, goober pea, monkey nut, or pindar; prompt on legume before mention]
6554318c4579e16ae2938e2e.answer_sanitized: peanuts [or groundnut, goober pea, monkey nut, or pindar; prompt on legume before mention]

6554318c4579e16ae2938e32.question: During a 2019 state visit to Europe, Xi Jinping expressed support for this action, and China has used the state-owned Poly Group Corporation to pursue similar actions involving the Old Summer Palace. John Lefas and Ed Vaizey lead a “Project” that campaigns for this action, which has led to a 2023 “hybrid” proposal from George Osborne. In 2014, Antonis Samaras declared, “the last … dogma about (*) immovability has ceased to exist” after one of the objects involved in this action was temporarily moved to Saint Petersburg. This action has been demanded ever since Thomas Bruce acquired and then sold a collection that includes a headless depiction of Ilissos and the head of a Horse of Selene. Other objects involved in this action include a caryatid from the (*) Erechtheion and a now-handless statue of Dionysus from the east pediment, which were created by Phidias. For 10 points, name this action that the British Museum refuses to do, which would lead to sculptures from the Parthenon being displayed in their home country.
6554318c4579e16ae2938e32.question_sanitized: During a 2019 state visit to Europe, Xi Jinping expressed support for this action, and China has used the state-owned Poly Group Corporation to pursue similar actions involving the Old Summer Palace. John Lefas and Ed Vaizey lead a "Project" that campaigns for this action, which has led to a 2023 "hybrid" proposal from George Osborne. In 2014, Antonis Samaras declared, "the last ... dogma about (*) immovability has ceased to exist" after one of the objects involved in this action was temporarily moved to Saint Petersburg. This action has been demanded ever since Thomas Bruce acquired and then sold a collection that includes a headless depiction of Ilissos and the head of a Horse of Selene. Other objects involved in this action include a caryatid from the (*) Erechtheion and a now-handless statue of Dionysus from the east pediment, which were created by Phidias. For 10 points, name this action that the British Museum refuses to do, which would lead to sculptures from the Parthenon being displayed in their home country.
6554318c4579e16ae2938e32.answer: returning the Elgin Marbles to Greece [accept answers describing both parts; accept answers mentioning sculptures from the Parthenon before mentioned] (In 2014, Ilissos was lent to the Hermitage.)
6554318c4579e16ae2938e32.answer_sanitized: returning the Elgin Marbles to Greece [accept answers describing both parts; accept answers mentioning sculptures from the Parthenon before mentioned] (In 2014, Ilissos was lent to the Hermitage.)

6554318c4579e16ae2938e37.question: A poem subtitled for this person ends with the image of “night breezes drum on the plantain leaf: / Let the plantain leaf take over the dance.” That poem dedicated to this person declares that “the time has come O Poet, / To decant your praise-names.” A six-line poem that responds to an essay by this author ends with “the jewel that always / outshines ordinary jewels, is your praise.” Another poem dedicated to this author is in the form of a Yoruba praise song and repeats, “They thought you would stop pursuing the (*) white elephant.” This author praised the “subtlety of rhythm” and “delicacies of colour” in a manuscript that he would close in trains and restaurants “lest some stranger would see how much it moved me.” The (*) centenary of this poet’s birth is celebrated in “Lament of the Masks” by Christopher Okigbo. This author wrote that “these prose translations … have stirred my blood as nothing has for years” in his preface to Gitanjali. For 10 points, a line of poetry from what author titles a novel about the yam farmer Okonkwo, Things Fall Apart?
6554318c4579e16ae2938e37.question_sanitized: A poem subtitled for this person ends with the image of "night breezes drum on the plantain leaf: / Let the plantain leaf take over the dance." That poem dedicated to this person declares that "the time has come O Poet, / To decant your praise-names." A six-line poem that responds to an essay by this author ends with "the jewel that always / outshines ordinary jewels, is your praise." Another poem dedicated to this author is in the form of a Yoruba praise song and repeats, "They thought you would stop pursuing the (*) white elephant." This author praised the "subtlety of rhythm" and "delicacies of colour" in a manuscript that he would close in trains and restaurants "lest some stranger would see how much it moved me." The (*) centenary of this poet's birth is celebrated in "Lament of the Masks" by Christopher Okigbo. This author wrote that "these prose translations ... have stirred my blood as nothing has for years" in his preface to Gitanjali. For 10 points, a line of poetry from what author titles a novel about the yam farmer Okonkwo, Things Fall Apart?
6554318c4579e16ae2938e37.answer: William Butler Yeats (the six-line poem is “To William Butler Yeats on Tagore” by Marianne Moore)
6554318c4579e16ae2938e37.answer_sanitized: William Butler Yeats (the six-line poem is "To William Butler Yeats on Tagore" by Marianne Moore)

6554318c4579e16ae2938e38.question: A member of this movement mocked writers with pen names like “Cold Ash” and “No Birth” and hoped “all writers become Fichtes [fick-TUHs] and Mazzinis.” That member of this movement argued that the West’s materialism made it more spiritual and used the translation “experimentalism” to promote the thought of his idol, John (*) Dewey. A member of this movement included “speak in the language of the time in which you live” and “speak only when you have something to say” in eight points of advice for writers. It’s not communism, but a member of this movement encouraged members to “destroy the old curiosity shop” of a central cultural figure and to replace him with “Mr. Science” and “Mr. Democracy.” The magazine La (*) Jeunesse or “New Youth” was the mouthpiece of this movement, which included Hú Shì and Chén Dúxiù [CHUN doo-SHYOH] and promoted the Doubting Antiquity school in historiography. For 10 points, name this Western-inspired philosophical and cultural movement, which helped spur the May 4th Movement and whose literary proponents included Lu Xun [SHUN].
6554318c4579e16ae2938e38.question_sanitized: A member of this movement mocked writers with pen names like "Cold Ash" and "No Birth" and hoped "all writers become Fichtes [fick-TUHs] and Mazzinis." That member of this movement argued that the West's materialism made it more spiritual and used the translation "experimentalism" to promote the thought of his idol, John (*) Dewey. A member of this movement included "speak in the language of the time in which you live" and "speak only when you have something to say" in eight points of advice for writers. It's not communism, but a member of this movement encouraged members to "destroy the old curiosity shop" of a central cultural figure and to replace him with "Mr. Science" and "Mr. Democracy." The magazine La (*) Jeunesse or "New Youth" was the mouthpiece of this movement, which included Hu Shi and Chen Duxiu [CHUN doo-SHYOH] and promoted the Doubting Antiquity school in historiography. For 10 points, name this Western-inspired philosophical and cultural movement, which helped spur the May 4th Movement and whose literary proponents included Lu Xun [SHUN].
6554318c4579e16ae2938e38.answer: New Culture Movement [or Xīn Wénhuà Yùndòng; prompt on May 4th Movement, Wǔsì Yùndong, Doubting Antiquity, or Yígǔpài with “which grew out of or was heavily influenced by what philosophical and literary movement?”; prompt on pragmatism I supposed since Hu Shih was also a pragmatist/experimentalist; prompt on nationalist or Chinese nationalist and equivalents] (the “old curiosity shop” figure is Confucius)
6554318c4579e16ae2938e38.answer_sanitized: New Culture Movement [or Xin Wenhua Yundong; prompt on May 4th Movement, Wusi Yundong, Doubting Antiquity, or Yigupai with "which grew out of or was heavily influenced by what philosophical and literary movement?"; prompt on pragmatism I supposed since Hu Shih was also a pragmatist/experimentalist; prompt on nationalist or Chinese nationalist and equivalents] (the "old curiosity shop" figure is Confucius)

6554318c4579e16ae2938e3b.question: The memoirs of Regina Salomea Pilsznytowa [pilsh-nee-taw-vah] describe her struggles establishing herself as a doctor in the capital of this state and her rivalries with doctors like Daniel de Fonseca. A Jewish merchant born in this state emigrated to Mumbai and became active in the opium trade, which in part funded his purchase of a mansion he named Sans Souci. Due to a prophecy that he would marry an “unchaste woman”, a religious leader in this state married a prostitute from (*) Livorno who had fled from the violence against Jews under Bohdan Khmelnytsky. French consul Ulysse de Ratti-Menton supported an accusation of blood libel in this state after the disappearance of the Italian Father Thomas. Persecution by a regional governor of this empire prompted (*) David Sassoon to move to the British Empire. Jewish immigration to this empire at the end of the 15th century prompted the development of the Ladino language. For 10 points, name this empire, which took in the largest proportion of Jews after the Alhambra Decree in cities like Sarajevo, Salonika, and Istanbul.

Persecution by a regional governor of this empire prompted (*) David Sassoon to move to the British Empire. Jewish immigration to this empire at the end of the 15th century prompted the development of the Ladino language. For 10 points, name this empire, which took in the largest proportion of Jews after the Alhambra Decree in cities like Sarajevo, Salonika, and Istanbul.
6554318c4579e16ae2938e3b.answer: Ottoman Empire [accept Devlet-i Aliye-i Osmaniye, Osmanlı Devleti, or Osmanlı İmparatorluğu; prompt on “Iraq”, “Mesopotamia”, “Greece”, or “Egypt” with “Which state had control of the region during this time?”]

6554318c4579e16ae2938e3c.question: A painting by this artist is analyzed in an essay that describes ten “resolutely alienated” men who watch with “childish, trancelike concentration,” while the audience is given a “beguiling rear view.” A painting by this artist bought by William Randolph Hearst depicts a leader on horseback with his army in the far background and was originally known as Oedipus. This artist’s trips to Egypt inspired a painting of evening prayer in Cairo and (*) Bonaparte Before the Sphinx. This artist’s son-in-law Aimé Morot helped create studies for a painting described as an “insistent, sexually charged mystery” in a Linda Nochlin essay about its “imaginary” subject matter. The Clark Art Institute holds that painting by this artist, as well as another in which a woman’s teeth are examined in a (*) slave market. In a painting by this artist, armed men sit against a blue-tiled wall and watch a nude boy with a reptile wrapped around his body. That painting was used for the front cover of Edward Said’s Orientalism. For 10 points, name this French academic painter of The Snake Charmer.

The Clark Art Institute holds that painting by this artist, as well as another in which a woman's teeth are examined in a (*) slave market. In a painting by this artist, armed men sit against a blue-tiled wall and watch a nude boy with a reptile wrapped around his body. That painting was used for the front cover of Edward Said's Orientalism. For 10 points, name this French academic painter of The Snake Charmer.
6554318c4579e16ae2938e3c.answer: Jean-Léon Gérôme (the Linda Nochlin essay is “The Imaginary Orient”)

6554318c4579e16ae2938e54.question: In one work, this character is told about a strategy that he reasons “cannot be entirely disreputable” because it was used by a man nicknamed “Agamemnon.” That story begins with this character receiving a “prescription” from Sayre that their new posting is not banishment. This character may have been based on the Scottish merchant Thomas Blake Glover. In another work, this character admits that he is a coward and sings farewell to a (*) “flowery refuge.” That role was premiered by tenor Giovanni Zenatello alongside Rosina Storchio. This character receives a telegram from the “blonde woman” asking “Can’t we have him at once?” in a story by John (*) Luther Long. This navy lieutenant, his second wife Kate, and the consul Sharpless are too late to stop the suicide of a woman who longs for this character in the aria “Un bel dì, vedremo.” For 10 points, name this operatic character, a symbol of American imperialism in Madame Butterfly.

This character receives a telegram from the "blonde woman" asking "Can't we have him at once?" in a story by John (*) Luther Long. This navy lieutenant, his second wife Kate, and the consul Sharpless are too late to stop the suicide of a woman who longs for this character in the aria "Un bel di, vedremo." For 10 points, name this operatic character, a symbol of American imperialism in Madame Butterfly.
6554318c4579e16ae2938e54.answer: B.F. Pinkerton [or Benjamin Franklin Pinkerton; prompt on Benjamin or Franklin; prompt on descriptions like the American in Madame Butterfly]

6554318d4579e16ae2938e64.question: This ruler’s forces captured the “ship” Diamond Rock, an actual rock fortified to blockade a Caribbean harbor. This ruler secured a trade relationship with Ghālib ibn Musā‘id by promising to revive grain shipments to his territory. A letter from this man to another ruler claimed that he had a “vast and invincible army, filled with the desire to save you from the iron yoke” of another power, but it was intercepted at (*) Muscat. Though its terms were never enacted, this ruler signed the Treaty of Finckenstein with Fath Ali Shah. De jure control was transferred [emphasize] to this man’s country, then to another country, within two days as part of a ceremony remembered as (*) Three Flags Day during a land sale under his rule. A campaign initiated by this man ended when his subordinate Jacques-François de Menou surrendered in the Capitulation of Alexandria. For 10 points, who was this man, who sought to disrupt British colonial interests in South Asia with his 1798 invasion of Egypt?

Though its terms were never enacted, this ruler signed the Treaty of Finckenstein with Fath Ali Shah. A campaign initiated by this man ended when his subordinate Jacques-Francois de Menou surrendered in the Capitulation of Alexandria. For 10 points, who was this man, who sought to disrupt British colonial interests in South Asia with his 1798 invasion of Egypt?
6554318d4579e16ae2938e64.answer: Napoleon Bonaparte [accept Napoléon Bonaparte; accept Napoleon I; accept Napoleone Buonaparte; prompt on Bonaparte]

6554318d4579e16ae2938e65.question: Note to players: Even though not all clues directly point to them, to specific subsets of this tossup’s answerline will also be accepted outright instead of anti-prompted. Frême tries to escape slavery with his white lover Marie in a work set in this archipelago by Louis Timogène Houat [ti-moh-ZHEN oo-WAH]. With a collection [emphasize] titled for elsewhere, a poet born in [emphasize] this archipelago warns “Beware the whites, inhabitants of the shore” but notes that “the rain fought for us” in poems attacking colonialism in the voice of the chief Ampanani. A title character of a novel set in this (*) archipelago elects to die in a shipwreck because swimming to shore would require taking off her clothes. The classic romance Paul et [ay] Virginie is set in this archipelago. One of these islands was the birthplace of a poet Pushkin called “my master,” Évariste de Parny, and of the influential Parnassian Leconte de Lisle [LEEL]. A Nobel Laureate from one of these islands fictionalized his research into his family’s involvement in slavery in it in his most recent novel, Alma. That author from this (*) archipelago is best-known for a “puzzle novel” centered on the amnesiac rapist Adam Pollo. For 10 points, name this archipelago that includes the birthplace of the author of Le Procès-verbal [PRO-say vair-BAL], J.M.G. Le Clézio.

That author from this (*) archipelago is best-known for a "puzzle novel" centered on the amnesiac rapist Adam Pollo. For 10 points, name this archipelago that includes the birthplace of the author of Le Proces-verbal [PRO-say vair-BAL], J.M.G. Le Clezio.
6554318d4579e16ae2938e65.answer: Mascarene Islands [or Islas Mascarenhas; accept Mauritius; acceptÎle de Réunion or its former name Île de Bourbon] (Mauritius is the setting of Paul et Virginie and birthplace of Le Clézio; everything else is Réunnionais; Leconte de Lisle doesn’t have any actual content represented and the second sentence refers to de Parny’s Chansons Madécasses)

6554318d4579e16ae2938e69.question: This author used the staccato lines “walls, town, port, refuge, from death” to open a poem in which the title beings attack his house. That poem appears in an “impractical book of pure poetry” in support of Greek Independence, Orientales. This author forgot where bonsai is from in a passage that introduces a group that “worked on man as the Chinese worked on trees.” In a novel by this author, the (*) narrator is captured by the maroon general Biassou after the Haitian Revolution starts during his wedding, but is saved and reunited with his wife by the title slave leader. This author, who coined the term “Comprachico,” told the abolitionist Victor Schoelcher “God offers Africa to Europe. Take it.” This author of (*) Bug-Jargal included “Algeria too harshly conquered” among political grievances in an interlude set in 1830 that begins “revolutions have a terrible arm and a happy hand.” One of this author’s title characters travels with the carnival vendor Ursus and the wolf Homo. For 10 points name this author, who satirized the reign of Louis-Philippe in The Man Who Laughs.

This author, who coined the term "Comprachico," told the abolitionist Victor Schoelcher "God offers Africa to Europe. Take it." This author of (*) Bug-Jargal included "Algeria too harshly conquered" among political grievances in an interlude set in 1830 that begins "revolutions have a terrible arm and a happy hand." One of this author's title characters travels with the carnival vendor Ursus and the wolf Homo. For 10 points name this author, who satirized the reign of Louis-Philippe in The Man Who Laughs.

6554318d4579e16ae2938e70.question: In an opera by this composer, Osmino plots to kill the sultan of Damietta by arming Christian prisoners. During the premiere of another opera by this composer, workers below the stage turned a wheel to shake the stage and simulate an Act 3 shipwreck, which is caused by a slave navigating directly into a storm. This composer created the knight Armando for his last Italian opera, (*) The Crusader in Egypt. A 2018 critical edition of this composer’s final opera revealed the many changes made by François-Joseph Fétis for its Paris premiere, such as moving the central setting to Madagascar. In this composer’s original version of that opera, a man in love with Inès is almost forced to marry an Indian princess, who later commits suidide by inhaling poisonous fumes from the (*) manchineel tree. Other characters from that opera include Nélusko, who sings a ballad about the sea giant Adamastor, and an explorer who sings the aria “O Paradis!” For 10 points, name this composer who depicted the adventures of Vasco da Gama in the grand opera L’Africaine [lah-free-ken].
6554318d4579e16ae2938e70.question_sanitized: In an opera by this composer, Osmino plots to kill the sultan of Damietta by arming Christian prisoners. This composer created the knight Armando for his last Italian opera, (*) The Crusader in Egypt. A 2018 critical edition of this composer's final opera revealed the many changes made by Francois-Joseph Fetis for its Paris premiere, such as moving the central setting to Madagascar. In this composer's original version of that opera, a man in love with Ines is almost forced to marry an Indian princess, who later commits suidide by inhaling poisonous fumes from the (*) manchineel tree. Other characters from that opera include Nelusko, who sings a ballad about the sea giant Adamastor, and an explorer who sings the aria "O Paradis!" For 10 points, name this composer who depicted the adventures of Vasco da Gama in the grand opera L'Africaine [lah-free-ken].
6554318d4579e16ae2938e70.answer: Giacomo Meyerbeer [or Jakob Liebmann Beer] (Meyerbeer’s original title was Vasco da Gama)
6554318d4579e16ae2938e70.answer_sanitized: Giacomo Meyerbeer [or Jakob Liebmann Beer] (Meyerbeer's original title was Vasco da Gama)

6554318d4579e16ae2938e72.question: A 1999 review of this novel begins by describing diplomat Agus Salim smoking a cigarette and notes that D.H. Lawrence called this novel “a most irritating work.” In this novel’s epigraph, a man named Lothario stands trial for cutting up Betsy into little pieces and salting them. During a “monotonous” story in this novel, a boy who is saved from a tiger attack by his family’s new buffalo later dies “embracing” bayonets after finding the mutilated corpse of his lover (*) Adinda. After Slotering is poisoned, this novel’s protagonist and his wife Tina are forced to leave Lebak by Mr. Slymering. This novel was written by an author who adopted a pen name that is Latin for “I have suffered much.” A [emphasize] later author who wrote the (*) Buru Quartet praised this novel as “The Book That Killed Colonialism” for exposing the abuses of the “Cultivation System.” This novel begins with Drystubble declaring, “I am a coffee-broker, and live at No. 37 Laurier Canal, Amsterdam.” For 10 points, name this novel criticizing Dutch colonialism in Indonesia, a work by Multatuli.

6554318d4579e16ae2938e7b.question: Emanuel Leutze created six paintings depicting this man’s life, including one in which he stands before two rulers as a monk and a boy unveil a chart and a flag behind him. For an exhibition subtitled for this person and “wohs” (woes), Jaune Quick-to-See Smith created a collage titled Trade featuring a canoe with a clothesline of sports merchandise above it. (*) Edward Sorin commissioned Luigi Gregori to paint 12 murals depicting this man’s life at the Main Building of Notre Dame. Although there are no contemporary portraits of this man, the most famous posthumous portrait was created by (*) Sebastiano del Piombo. A large surrealist painting titled for this man unusually features the Catalan flag and references the spears in The Surrender of Breda with numerous tall crucifixes that dominate the right side. In that painting, this man holds a banner that features the artist’s wife Gala as the Virgin Mary as he drags a boat up a beach. For 10 points, name this explorer whom Salvador Dalí depicted during his “Discovery of America.”

6554318d4579e16ae2938e7f.question: An architect from this country who studied at Cornell designed the neoclassical St. La Salle Hall for a university in its capital. That architect from this country later used an Art Deco design for his namesake Mapúa Mansion. In this country, hundreds of immigrant workers died building the Kennon Road. William Cameron Forbes commissioned William E. Parsons to design a white “Mansion House” that is now used as this country’s presidential summer palace. A leader of the (*) City Beautiful movement expanded this country’s capital in a 1905 plan that added new radiating “arteries” and expanded its tranvía streetcar system. In How to Hide an Empire, Daniel Immerwahr discusses how in this country, the designer of the (*) White City was an “architectural imperialist” who operated without local approval. Land seized from this country’s Igorot people was used by the Insular Government to build new cities. For 10 points, name this country where Daniel Burnham designed the “summer capital” Baguio [BAH-ghee-oh] in the tropical pine forests of Luzon.
6554318d4579e16ae2938e7f.question_sanitized: An architect from this country who studied at Cornell designed the neoclassical St. La Salle Hall for a university in its capital. That architect from this country later used an Art Deco design for his namesake Mapua Mansion. In this country, hundreds of immigrant workers died building the Kennon Road. William Cameron Forbes commissioned William E. Parsons to design a white "Mansion House" that is now used as this country's presidential summer palace. A leader of the (*) City Beautiful movement expanded this country's capital in a 1905 plan that added new radiating "arteries" and expanded its tranvia streetcar system. In How to Hide an Empire, Daniel Immerwahr discusses how in this country, the designer of the (*) White City was an "architectural imperialist" who operated without local approval. Land seized from this country's Igorot people was used by the Insular Government to build new cities. For 10 points, name this country where Daniel Burnham designed the "summer capital" Baguio [BAH-ghee-oh] in the tropical pine forests of Luzon.
6554318d4579e16ae2938e7f.answer: Philippines [or Republic of the Philippines] (the first two lines refer to Tomás Mapúa)
6554318d4579e16ae2938e7f.answer_sanitized: Philippines [or Republic of the Philippines] (the first two lines refer to Tomas Mapua)

6554318d4579e16ae2938e88.question: Most of the non-still life drawings in Douglas Hamilton’s illustrated autobiography depict this practice. John Guille Millais, son of artist John Everett, was a founding member of the SPWFE, an organization that advocated for policies that protected this practice. Paul Rainey’s 1912 film of him engaging in this practice was the first [emphasize] commercially successful depiction of it on film. (*) Thomas Austin is best known for the unintended consequences that resulted from his attempt to promote this practice in Victoria. The Raja of Benares nearly had part of his land confiscated in 1839 because he did not engage in one type of this practice for four years. Englishmen engaging in this practice in South Asia often did so with the assistance of (*) shikaris. An account of engaging in this practice in Kumaon was written by Jim Corbett, who became famous for this practice prior to advocating for conservation. For 10 points, what is this practice, in which the “Big Five” might be pursued for sport?

6554318d4579e16ae2938e8b.question: This philosopher dismissed critics of his racial theories for not showing why “the happy inhabitants of Tahiti” should “exist at all.” Robert Bernasconi pointed out that this thinker’s reliance on James Tobin to support his anti-Black racism meant he ignored James Ramsay’s refutations in the same periodical. This thinker adapted a Galenic four-humor theory into a four-level (*) racial hierarchy with Native Americans at the bottom and had several debates with the scientific racist Georg Forster. Defenders of this thinker like Pauline Kleingeld argue that he rejected racism in a work that claims that, unlike American “savages,” European ones “know a better way of using the vanquished than by eating them.” This author of (*) Anthropology from a Practical Point of View wrote a work that decries the “inhospitable behavior of civilised nations” in colonial wars and the slave trade. That work proposes a system whose first “definitive article” is “the civil constitution of each state shall be Republican.” For 10 points, name this philosopher, who sort of condemned imperialism in Perpetual Peace.

Defenders of this thinker like Pauline Kleingeld argue that he rejected racism in a work that claims that, unlike American "savages," European ones "know a better way of using the vanquished than by eating them." This author of (*) Anthropology from a Practical Point of View wrote a work that decries the "inhospitable behavior of civilised nations" in colonial wars and the slave trade. That work proposes a system whose first "definitive article" is "the civil constitution of each state shall be Republican." For 10 points, name this philosopher, who sort of condemned imperialism in Perpetual Peace.

6554318d4579e16ae2938e90.question: The Inuit statesman Tagak Curley received an apology from this author’s great-grandson for “150 years” of pain caused by a play he edited. In Culture and Imperialism, Edward Said used the belief of a character created by this author that “the earth was made for them to trade in, and the sun and moon was made to give them light” to illustrate the “overweening self-importance” created by imperial markets. This author heavily edited the play The (*) Frozen Deep for Wilkie Collins, specifically to shift blame for the Franklin Expedition to the Inuit. This author’s distaste for George Catlin caused this author to write a racist essay sarcastically titled “The Noble Savage.” The fictional Borrioboola-Gha people benefit from the “telescopic philanthropy” of a character created by this author. This (*) author’s observations on slavery in the travelogue American Notes provided materials for a novel by this creator of Ms. Jellyby whose title character is apprenticed to Seth Pecksniff. For 10 points, name this author, who often centrally featured colonial trading firms in novels like Dombey and Son.

The fictional Borrioboola-Gha people benefit from the "telescopic philanthropy" of a character created by this author. For 10 points, name this author, who often centrally featured colonial trading firms in novels like Dombey and Son.
6554318d4579e16ae2938e90.answer: Charles Dickens [prompt on Wilkie Collins until mentioned with “who worked on Frozen Deep with what other author as editor?”]

6554318d4579e16ae2938e95.question: A short piece inspired by these ensembles begins with a 2/4 Vivace theme that starts with the notes “grace note E-F-D-D-D.” A composer’s Op. 76 imitates one of these ensembles passing by with six variations on a B-flat major theme, which was used for a play in which Athena wakes up from a thousand year sleep. These ensembles inspired a 45-bar section with a vocal solo that begins with two dotted half notes on the word “Froh” and tells (*) “brothers” to “run … joyfully, like a conquering hero.” These ensembles are imitated in the seventh “Ode to Joy” variation in Beethoven 9, which features triangle and a prominent tenor solo. A theme inspired by these ensembles begins with the quick notes (*) “B-A-G-sharp-A-C”, which is then repeated a third higher. These ensembles inspired the nickname of Mozart’s Violin Concerto No. 5, and percussion is used to imitate them in The Abduction from the Seraglio. For 10 points, name these ensembles that inspired the third movement of Mozart’s Rondo alla Turca, whose core was often elite soldiers kidnapped as Christians.

6554318e4579e16ae2938e98.question: A composer wrote that this musical style “makes our tonic and dominant seem like empty phantoms for the use of unwise infants.” John Cranko choreographed a ballet inspired by this musical style that was originally titled The Green Serpent and features a quartet of flying frogs. A piano movement imitating this style uses the ascending motif “G-sharp, C-sharp, D-sharp” and is marked presque sans nuance. This musical style inspired a piano suite by Leopold (*) Godowsky, as well as a Benjamin Britten ballet in which Belle Rose is blindfolded and dances with the title prince, who first appears as a salamander. A rhapsody imitating this style by Bourgault-Ducoudray was performed at the (*) 1889 Exposition Universelle. That piece inspired a piano movement in this style that is followed by “Evening in Granada” and “Gardens in the Rain” in the Estampes suite by Debussy. For 10 points, name this Asian musical style based on pitched metal percussion.
6554318e4579e16ae2938e98.question_sanitized: A composer wrote that this musical style "makes our tonic and dominant seem like empty phantoms for the use of unwise infants." John Cranko choreographed a ballet inspired by this musical style that was originally titled The Green Serpent and features a quartet of flying frogs. A piano movement imitating this style uses the ascending motif "G-sharp, C-sharp, D-sharp" and is marked presque sans nuance. This musical style inspired a piano suite by Leopold (*) Godowsky, as well as a Benjamin Britten ballet in which Belle Rose is blindfolded and dances with the title prince, who first appears as a salamander. A rhapsody imitating this style by Bourgault-Ducoudray was performed at the (*) 1889 Exposition Universelle. That piece inspired a piano movement in this style that is followed by "Evening in Granada" and "Gardens in the Rain" in the Estampes suite by Debussy. For 10 points, name this Asian musical style based on pitched metal percussion.
6554318e4579e16ae2938e98.answer: gamelan [or gambelan; prompt on Indonesian or Javanese music] (the first line quote is from Debussy, the Godowsky piece is his Java Suite, and the Britten ballet is The Prince of the Pagodas)
